title: EIP-8025: Optional Execution Proofs
description: Introducing optional execution proofs on the consensus layer
author: Kevaundray Wedderburn (@kevaundray), Justin Drake (@JustinDrake) <justin@ethereum.org>
laymanDescription: Normally every validator must replay each block's transactions against the full Ethereum state to check it is valid - costly work that grows with the gas limit. This feature lets volunteer nodes produce small cryptographic proofs that others can verify cheaply, without holding the state. It is fully opt-in and changes no consensus rules.

scaleL1.description: Stateless, sublinear payload verification decouples a node's validation cost from the gas limit and state size, eventually supporting a higher-throughput L1 without raising validator hardware requirements.

endUsers.description: No direct impact; the mechanism is opt-in and changes no consensus rules, transaction behaviour, or fees.

appDevs.description: No impact; introduces no new opcodes, gas changes, or contract-level semantics.

walletDevs.description: No impact; no transaction format or RPC changes affecting wallets.

toolingInfra.description: New proof-node and prover infrastructure, plus gossip and req/resp tooling, needed to generate, serve, and verify execution proofs.

layer2s.description: L2s reusing the maturing stateless guest, witness format, and proving stack could benefit, though nothing is required of them.

stakersNodes.description: Opt-in: proof-verifying nodes gain additional stateless validation; proof-generating nodes take on extra bandwidth and proving costs.

clClients.description: Substantial opt-in work: new gossip topic, ProofEngine interface, req/resp protocols, ENR key, and an out-of-state-transition proof handler.

elClients.description: Implement the stateless guest program, execution witness construction, SSZ stateless input/output schema, and host-side input assembly.

benefits: Stateless, sublinear verification decouples node cost from gas limit and state size
First step towards lowering the hardware floor for validators, broadening participation and decentralisation
Fully opt-in: no consensus rule changes required
Lets the proving stack mature under mainnet conditions & allows for gathering data and surface unknown unknowns without putting any load-bearing part of the network at risk
tradeoffs: No incentives for proof-generating nodes; relies on altruistic provers
Adds gossip bandwidth and client implementation complexity for opted-in nodes
